of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]] from 784 to 794. Its location was in [[:en:Otokuni District, Kyoto|Otokuni District]], [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]], corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and [[:en:Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto]], the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the [[:en:Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyō-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodo River]]. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa-tsu]] on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards [[:en:Lake Biwa|Lake Biwa]] an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with [[:en:Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu]] on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''[[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]]'' immigrant clans of southern [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]] from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the [[:en:Naniwa-kyō|Naniwa-kyō]] palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial [[:en:Prince Sawara|Prince Sawara]], was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]], and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato daigoku-den-2.jpg|Daigokuden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ko-den.JPG|Goden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ho-do.JPG|宝幢（復元） File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato choudo-in.jpg|Site of the Chodo-in File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato chodo-in nishi-daiyon-dou-2.jpg|Chodo-in West Fourth Hall Ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato kaisho-mon.JPG|Chodoin Kaishomon File:Daigokuden of Nagaoka palace.jpg|Reconstruction </gallery>

{{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|WD=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato daigoku-den-2.jpg|Daigokuden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ko-den.JPG|Goden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ho-do.JPG|宝幢（復元） File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato choudo-in.jpg|Site of the Chodo-in File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato chodo-in nishi-daiyon-dou-2.jpg|Chodo-in West Fourth Hall Ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato kaisho-mon.JPG|Chodoin Kaishomon File:Daigokuden of Nagaoka palace.jpg|Reconstruction </gallery>

Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|WD=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|WD=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato daigoku-den-2.jpg|Daigokuden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ko-den.JPG|Goden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ho-do.JPG|宝幢（復元） File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato choudo-in.jpg|Site of the Chodo-in File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato chodo-in nishi-daiyon-dou-2.jpg|Chodo-in West Fourth Hall Ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato kaisho-mon.JPG|Chodoin Kaishomon File:Daigokuden of Nagaoka palace.jpg|Reconstruction </gallery>

{{Short description|Historical capital of Japan}} {{About|the historical capital of Japan|the city in Kyoto Prefecture|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nagaoka-kyō | native_name =長岡京 | native_name_lang = | image =Nagaoka-kyu-ato sekihi-2.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Nagaoka Palace ruin monument in {{ill|Mukō|en|Mukō|WD=Q850640|lt=Mukō}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Nagaoka-kyō | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|region:JP-26|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō, Kyoto]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs ={{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} - {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|WD=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato daigoku-den-2.jpg|Daigokuden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ko-den.JPG|Goden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ho-do.JPG|宝幢（復元） File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato choudo-in.jpg|Site of the Chodo-in File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato chodo-in nishi-daiyon-dou-2.jpg|Chodo-in West Fourth Hall Ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato kaisho-mon.JPG|Chodoin Kaishomon File:Daigokuden of Nagaoka palace.jpg|Reconstruction </gallery>

Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Historical capital of Japan}} {{About|the historical capital of Japan|the city in Kyoto Prefecture|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nagaoka-kyō | native_name =長岡京 | native_name_lang = | image =Nagaoka-kyu-ato sekihi-2.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Nagaoka Palace ruin monument in {{ill|Mukō|en|Mukō|WD=Q850640|lt=Mukō|1=Mukō}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Nagaoka-kyō | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|region:JP-26|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō, Kyoto]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs ={{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}} - {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|WD=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District|1=Otokuni District, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|1=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|1=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō|1=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara|1=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato daigoku-den-2.jpg|Daigokuden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ko-den.JPG|Goden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ho-do.JPG|宝幢（復元） File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato choudo-in.jpg|Site of the Chodo-in File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato chodo-in nishi-daiyon-dou-2.jpg|Chodo-in West Fourth Hall Ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato kaisho-mon.JPG|Chodoin Kaishomon File:Daigokuden of Nagaoka palace.jpg|Reconstruction </gallery>

The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|1=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō|1=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara|1=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Historical capital of Japan}} {{About|the historical capital of Japan|the city in Kyoto Prefecture|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nagaoka-kyō | native_name =長岡京 | native_name_lang = | image =Nagaoka-kyu-ato sekihi-2.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Nagaoka Palace ruin monument in {{ill|Mukō|en|Mukō|WD=Q850640|lt=Mukō|1=Mukō}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Nagaoka-kyō | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|region:JP-26|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō, Kyoto]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs ={{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}} - {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|WD=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District|1=Otokuni District, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|1=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Omi Province|Omi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|1=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō|1=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara|1=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Historical capital of Japan}} {{About|the historical capital of Japan|the city in Kyoto Prefecture|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nagaoka-kyō | native_name =長岡京 | native_name_lang = | image =Nagaoka-kyu-ato sekihi-2.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Nagaoka Palace ruin monument in {{ill|Mukō|en|Mukō|WD=Q850640|lt=Mukō|1=Mukō}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Nagaoka-kyō | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|region:JP-26|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō, Kyoto]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs ={{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}} - {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|WD=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District|1=Otokuni District, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|WD=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|1=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|WD=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|1=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō|1=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|WD=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara|1=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato daigoku-den-2.jpg|Daigokuden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ko-den.JPG|Goden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ho-do.JPG|宝幢（復元） File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato choudo-in.jpg|Site of the Chodo-in File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato chodo-in nishi-daiyon-dou-2.jpg|Chodo-in West Fourth Hall Ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato kaisho-mon.JPG|Chodoin Kaishomon File:Daigokuden of Nagaoka palace.jpg|Reconstruction </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyoto)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyoto)|WD=Q20983065|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyoto)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyoto)}}

Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|WD=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Historical capital of Japan}} {{About|the historical capital of Japan|the city in Kyoto Prefecture|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nagaoka-kyō | native_name =長岡京 | native_name_lang = | image =Nagaoka-kyu-ato sekihi-2.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Nagaoka Palace ruin monument in {{ill|Mukō|en|Mukō|qid=Q850640|lt=Mukō|1=Mukō}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Nagaoka-kyō | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|region:JP-26|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō, Kyoto]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs ={{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}} - {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|56|36.6|N|135|42|11.4|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Nagaoka-kyō'''|長岡京}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|qid=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}} from 784 to 794. Its location was in {{ill|Otokuni District, Kyoto|en|Otokuni District, Kyoto|qid=Q376858|lt=Otokuni District|1=Otokuni District, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|qid=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}, corresponding to a 4.3 x 5.3 kilometer area spanning the borders of modern cities of [[:en:Mukō, Kyoto|Mukō]] and {{ill|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|en|Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|qid=Q861246|lt=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto|1=Nagaokakyō, Kyoto}}, the town of [[:en:Ōyamazaki|Ōyamazaki]], and the {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}} ward of the city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. The ruins of the palace have been found in the Kaidecho neighbourhood of the city of Mukō and have been designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1964, with the area under protection expanded in 2016. <ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1720|title=長岡京跡 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Overview== Nagaoka-kyō was built by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who instructed that the new capital be established 40 kilometers north of Heijō-kyō in an attempt to overcome Heijō-kyō's various geographical weaknesses. Nagaoka-kyō, was situated at the confluence point of three major rivers, where the Katsura River and the Uji River, formed the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|qid=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}}. A port called Yamazakitsu was established to unload goods from all over the country, where they were transferred to small ships. This made it easy to transport supplies efficiently by boat to Nagaoka-kyō, whereas Heijō-kyō could only be reached by land routes. In addition, Ogura Pond which existed at the time to the southeast of Nagaoka-kyō expected to be used as a logistics base. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have revealed that almost every house in Nagaoka-kyō had a well, and measures were also taken to deal with sewage, both of which had been problems in Heijō-kyō. Running water from the side of the road was drawn into houses to flush away excrement. The natural spring water from the northwest of Nagaoka-kyō naturally flowed southeast through the city, which was built on a gentle slope, and this washed away filth into the river, keeping the city clean. The imperial palace was situated on a minor plateau 15 meters higher than the city, visually demonstrating the authority of the emperor, whereas Heijō-kyō was completely flat. Furthermore, the main seaport of {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|qid=Q861618|lt=Naniwa-tsu|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}} on the [[Seto Inland Sea]] has silted up and was increasingly unusable. The location of Nagaoka-kyō facilities the development of a new route via the [[Kanzaki River]] towards {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|qid=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} an [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The new capital was also intended to rectify Heijō-kyō's various political weaknesses. Per the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', Emperor Kanmu consulted closely with {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|qid=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|1=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}} on the location of the new capital. Nagaoka happened to Tanetsugu's family home, and a strong support base for the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Other reasons included a desire for distance from the increasingly politicized clergy of the great Buddhist establishments in Heijō-kyō, and a desire to improve relations with the economically powerful ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|qid=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}}'' immigrant clans of southern {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|qid=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}} from whom his mother was descended.
New Year's ceremonies were held at the Nagaoka-kyō palace on New Year's Day in 785, which means that the palace was completed only six months after construction of the capital began. The construction of the palace was undertaken over with consideration to opposition from the people of Heijō-kyō to the relocation of the capital. At that time, it was common for palaces to be built by demolishing the original structures and relocating the buildings to the new site; however, in the case of Nagaoka-kyō, the structures of Heijō-kyō were left intact in situ, and the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|qid=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa-kyō|1=Naniwa-kyō}} palace was relocated instead. Nonetheless, in September of the same year, Fujiwara no Tanetsugu was assassinated, and several officials associated with the great state temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] were implicated. Emperor Kanmu's younger brother, Imperial {{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|qid=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara|1=Prince Sawara}}, was also imprisoned and exiled because of his strong opposition to the move of the capital. The prince died while en route to his exile, still harboring a grudge. After his death, various incidents occurred in 792, such as famine and epidemics due to drought, the successive deaths of the Empress and other close relatives of Emperor Kanmu, arson of the main hall of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and the onset of illness of the Crown Prince. The following year, an ''[[onmyōji]]'' declared that these disasters were caused by the ''[[onryō]]'', or vengeful spirit of Prince Sawara. Despite efforts at appeasing the prince's spirit, heavy rains followed by floods caused great damage to Nagaoka-kyō. {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|qid=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}}, the aristocrat in charge of flood control proposed relocation of the capital. A new site was selected to the northeast in 793 and the capital officially relocated to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} in 794.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
After the transfer of the capital, the former Nagaoka-kyō area became the domain of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, and the Nagaoka Tenman-gu {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} was constructed after his exile in 901. Although the name of "Nagaoka" survived as a place name, the actual site of the palace was lost for many centuries. The site of the palace was only re-discovered in 1954 and extensive archaeological excavations have been conducted since.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato daigoku-den-2.jpg|Daigokuden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ko-den.JPG|Goden ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato ho-do.JPG|宝幢（復元） File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato choudo-in.jpg|Site of the Chodo-in File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato chodo-in nishi-daiyon-dou-2.jpg|Chodo-in West Fourth Hall Ruins File:Nagaoka-kyu-ato kaisho-mon.JPG|Chodoin Kaishomon File:Daigokuden of Nagaoka palace.jpg|Reconstruction </gallery>
